title: 32-Year-Old GNU Telnetd Buffer Overflow Enables Unauthenticated Root RCE Before Login Prompt: CVE-2026-32746 Affects FreeBSD, NetBSD, Citrix NetScaler, TrueNAS and 3,362 Internet-Exposed Hosts With No Patch Available Until April 2026
affected_products: GNU InetUtils telnetd all versions through 2.7
Most Linux distributions using GNU inetutils telnetd

sources.name: Dream Security Advisory: Pre-Auth Remote Code Execution via Buffer Overflow in Telnetd LINEMODE SLC Handler
watchTowr Labs: A 32-Year-Old Bug Walks into a Telnet Server — GNU InetUtils Telnetd CVE-2026-32746
Censys Advisory: CVE-2026-32746 Internet Exposure Analysis
GNU bug-inetutils Mailing List: CVE-2026-32746 Patch Discussion
The Hacker News: Critical Telnetd Flaw CVE-2026-32746 Affects Multiple Unix Systems
Security Affairs: Researchers Warn of Unpatched Critical Telnetd Flaw Affecting All Versions
CSO Online: Telnet Vulnerability Opens Door to Remote Code Execution as Root
Industrial Cyber: Dream Security Flags Critical RCE in GNU InetUtils Telnetd Exposing ICS and OT Systems
CyCognito Blog: Emerging Threat GNU InetUtils Telnetd LINEMODE SLC Buffer Overflow CVE-2026-32746
Vulert: CVE-2026-32746 Telnetd Unauthenticated Root RCE Analysis
Rescana: CVE-2026-32746 Critical Unpatched Vulnerability in GNU InetUtils Telnetd
GitHub PoC and Detection Tool: jeffaf/cve-2026-32746
Cibersafety: CVE-2026-32746 GNU InetUtils Telnetd Analysis

timeline.event: The bounds-checking vulnerability is introduced in the LINEMODE SLC handler code of GNU telnetd — a coding defect that would survive undetected for over 32 years across countless distributions and derivative implementations
Dream Security researchers discover and disclose CVE-2026-32746 to the GNU InetUtils maintainers; CVE ID assigned; vendor notified with full technical details of the LINEMODE SLC buffer overflow

watchTowr Labs publishes comprehensive technical analysis and proof-of-concept exploit demonstrating pre-authentication RCE as root via crafted SLC triplet overflow; public disclosure coincides with GNU advisory
CVE-2026-32746 published in NVD with CVSS 9.8 Critical rating; major news outlets including The Hacker News, CSO Online, and Security Affairs cover the disclosure
Multiple vendors (FreeBSD, NetBSD, Citrix, TrueNAS) confirm applicability of CVE-2026-32746 to their telnetd implementations; independent analysis confirms BSDs and embedded systems are affected
Censys publishes internet-wide scan results identifying approximately 3,362 publicly exposed telnetd instances vulnerable to CVE-2026-32746; ICS/OT system exposure highlighted by Industrial Cyber
Expected patch release date for GNU InetUtils and major downstream distributions; no official patch available as of March 24, 2026

simple_summary.what_happened: Security researchers at Dream Security discovered a critical 32-year-old programming error in GNU telnetd — the software that serves Telnet remote access connections on Linux, BSD, and many embedded systems. The bug exists in the code that handles 'LINEMODE SLC' option negotiation during a telnet connection handshake, and allows an attacker to overflow a fixed-size memory buffer before the server ever asks for a username or password. By sending a specially crafted sequence of data during the initial connection, an attacker can execute arbitrary code on the server with root privileges. As of March 24, 2026, no official patch exists (expected around April 1), meaning approximately 3,362 internet-facing hosts remain immediately vulnerable.
simple_summary.who_is_affected: Any system running GNU InetUtils telnetd version 2.7 or earlier is vulnerable — this includes most Linux distributions, FreeBSD, NetBSD, DragonFlyBSD, Citrix NetScaler appliances, TrueNAS Core NAS systems, Haiku OS, uCLinux, and libmtev-based systems. While telnet is largely deprecated in modern internet-facing infrastructure, it remains widely used in: Industrial Control Systems (ICS/SCADA networks), legacy enterprise equipment, network appliances, embedded devices, internal lab environments, and NAS/storage systems. Censys identified ~3,362 internet-exposed vulnerable hosts; internal network exposure is likely far higher.
simple_summary.what_attackers_do: Step 1: Attacker opens a TCP connection to port 23 (telnet) on the target system — this requires no credentials or prior access, just network connectivity to the port. Step 2: During the initial Telnet option negotiation phase (before any login prompt appears), the attacker sends a WILL LINEMODE message to indicate it supports line-mode editing. Step 3: The server responds accepting LINEMODE, at which point the attacker sends a malicious SLC (Set Local Characters) suboption containing 40 or more triplets with invalid function codes — far exceeding the 108-byte buffer allocated in the add_slc() function. Step 4: The oversized payload overflows the buffer, corrupting approximately 400 bytes of adjacent BSS segment memory including the slcptr pointer variable. Step 5: When the server subsequently calls end_slc() using the corrupted slcptr, the attacker achieves an arbitrary memory write primitive, leading to complete root-level code execution on the server with full system control — all before the server ever displayed a login prompt.
what_you_should_do: Immediately disable the telnet daemon (telnetd) service on all systems where it is not strictly required — replace with SSH for remote access
If telnet cannot be disabled, implement network-level access controls (firewall rules, ACLs) to restrict port 23 access to only explicitly trusted IP addresses
Scan your network for systems with telnet (port 23) open and prioritize patching or disabling on internet-facing and ICS/OT systems
Monitor for the patch release from GNU InetUtils (expected ~April 1, 2026) and your operating system vendors, then apply immediately upon availability
Deploy IDS/IPS rules to detect exploitation attempts via oversized SLC suboption payloads in telnet traffic
simple_summary.why_this_is_serious: The combination of a 32-year bug history, pre-authentication exploitation (attacker wins before you can verify identity), root-level code execution impact, and no available patch makes CVE-2026-32746 exceptionally dangerous for any system still running telnet. ICS and OT environments are particularly at risk since they often rely on legacy telnet-based management and cannot be easily patched or taken offline. The availability of public proof-of-concept exploit code and detection tools from watchTowr Labs means exploitation tools are in widespread hands, and opportunistic attackers are actively scanning.

tactics.description: CVE-2026-32746 is exploited by sending a single crafted TCP connection to port 23 on any system running vulnerable GNU telnetd, requiring only network connectivity to the target — no credentials, no prior access, no user interaction

The BSS buffer overflow in add_slc() corrupts the slcptr pointer in telnetd memory, which when used by end_slc() produces an arbitrary write primitive that is leveraged for remote code execution as the telnetd process user (typically root)

GNU telnetd typically runs as root on Linux/BSD systems, meaning the initial exploitation directly achieves root/system privileges without any additional privilege escalation step — a single exploit provides maximum access

tactics.technique_name: Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools
tactics.description: Exploitation occurs during pre-authentication negotiation phase, bypassing all authentication mechanisms and audit logging that typically captures login attempts. Many legacy systems lack telnet connection anomaly detection.

With root access achieved via telnet exploitation, attackers can install SSH keys or modify authentication mechanisms to establish persistent, stealthier access and move laterally to other systems on the network

detection_guidance.summary: Detection of CVE-2026-32746 exploitation is possible at the network level by monitoring telnet (port 23) traffic for malformed SLC suboption sequences. watchTowr Labs published a non-destructive detection script that distinguishes patched from vulnerable servers based on how they respond to oversized SLC triplet sequences. Organizations should prioritize asset discovery to identify all telnet-enabled systems before exploitation occurs.

log_sources.description: Monitor for IAC SB LINEMODE 0x03 (SLC suboption) sequences in telnet traffic with payload sizes exceeding 90 bytes. Legitimate LINEMODE SLC negotiation uses at most a handful of triplets; 40+ triplets in a suboption is a clear exploitation indicator.

Log all inbound connections to TCP port 23. Identify any internet-facing systems with port 23 open — these should be treated as immediate priority for remediation. Track connection origins for threat intelligence correlation.

Monitor telnetd process for crash events (SIGSEGV, SIGABRT) which indicate exploitation attempts. Successful exploitation may not crash the process but may generate unusual telnetd memory allocation events.

Alert on any child processes spawned by telnetd that are not typical telnet session processes. Exploitation leading to shell access will show telnetd spawning /bin/sh, /bin/bash, or other unexpected commands.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Telnet SLC suboption payload exceeding 90 bytes or containing 40+ triplets
what_to_look_for.description: The exploit sends oversized SLC triplet data to overflow the 108-byte slcbuf. Any SLC suboption larger than approximately 90 bytes is anomalous and indicative of an exploitation attempt against CVE-2026-32746.

what_to_look_for.indicator: Telnetd process spawning unexpected child processes
what_to_look_for.description: Successful exploitation causes telnetd to execute attacker-controlled code. Monitor for unusual child processes (bash, sh, nc, curl) spawned by telnetd or its child login processes.
what_to_look_for.log_field: process.parent.name='telnetd' AND process.name NOT IN ('login', 'bash_login') AND event.type='exec'

what_to_look_for.indicator: Inbound TCP connection to port 23 from internet IP addresses
what_to_look_for.description: Any internet-originated connection to telnet port 23 should be treated as suspicious. Legitimate telnet use should be restricted to trusted internal networks; internet-facing telnet exposure indicates misconfiguration.
what_to_look_for.log_field: destination.port=23 AND network.direction='inbound' AND source.ip NOT IN private_ranges

what_to_look_for.indicator: Telnetd process crash or SIGSEGV signal
what_to_look_for.description: Failed exploitation attempts may cause telnetd to crash due to memory corruption. Sequential crash events from multiple source IPs indicate scanning/exploitation activity against CVE-2026-32746.

immediate_actions.action: Disable Telnet Service on All Non-Essential Systems
immediate_actions.description: Unless telnet is an absolute operational requirement, disable it immediately. For most systems, SSH is a fully capable and far more secure replacement. Run the appropriate disable commands for your OS. This is the most effective protection available before a patch exists.
immediate_actions.command: systemctl disable --now telnet.socket telnetd 2>/dev/null; update-inetd --disable telnet 2>/dev/null; echo 'Telnet service disabled'

immediate_actions.action: Block Telnet Port at Network Perimeter and Host Firewall
immediate_actions.description: If telnet cannot be disabled (e.g., required for ICS/OT operations), immediately restrict TCP port 23 access using firewall rules to only allow connections from explicitly trusted management IP addresses. This prevents internet-based exploitation while ICS operations continue.

immediate_actions.action: Audit Network for Internet-Exposed Telnet Instances
immediate_actions.description: Use internal network scanning to identify all systems with TCP port 23 open across your environment. Prioritize internet-facing hosts (confirmed by Censys scan showing 3,362 globally exposed), DMZ systems, OT/ICS networks, and network appliances including Citrix NetScaler and TrueNAS systems.

immediate_actions.action: Monitor for Patch Release and Apply Immediately
immediate_actions.description: Monitor GNU InetUtils project (https://www.gnu.org/software/inetutils/) and your OS vendor security advisories for the CVE-2026-32746 patch expected approximately April 1, 2026. Ensure you have alerting in place so the patch can be applied within 24 hours of release given the severity and public PoC availability.

immediate_actions.action: Deploy IDS Signatures for Telnet SLC Overflow Detection
immediate_actions.description: Configure IDS/IPS signatures to detect oversized SLC suboption payloads in telnet traffic. The watchTowr detection script (github.com/jeffaf/cve-2026-32746) can identify vulnerable hosts non-destructively. Suricata/Snort rules for large LINEMODE SLC negotiation sequences provide real-time detection.
